title: Fitting a compression fitting
subtitle: Cut clean, slide the olive, tighten correctly
excerpt: How to make a leak-free joint on copper or plastic pipe using a compression fitting: cutting the pipe square, fitting the olive and nut in the correct order, and tightening to the precise amount needed without over- or under-compressing the olive.
sourceNotes: Standard UK plumbing practice. CIPHE technical guidance. Compression fitting tightening specifications from BSI BS EN 1254 (copper fittings for water and heating). Olive compression guidance from Pegler Yorkshire and Conex technical data.

recipeTools.notes: For holding the fitting body while tightening the nut.

aliases: how to fit a compression joint

glossaryTerms.definition: A plumbing joint sealed by tightening a nut that compresses a brass olive ring onto the pipe. No heat required; serviceable. The default for radiator valves and isolating valves.

content.text: A
compression fitting

is the plumbing joint anyone can make without heat or specialist tools. The brass olive is compressed onto the pipe by the nut and forms a watertight seal. The critical variable is tightening: a nut tightened too little leaks; a nut tightened too much deforms the olive and leaks. The rule is hand-tight plus one and a quarter turns.
Components

A compression fitting has three parts: the body (which comes as an elbow, tee, straight coupling, or valve), the nut (which tightens over the pipe), and the olive (a brass or soft-metal ring that sits between the pipe and the fitting body). The nut drives the olive onto the pipe and into the fitting body seat as it is tightened. The components must go onto the pipe in the correct order before the pipe is inserted into the fitting: nut first, then olive, then insert pipe into fitting.
Preparation
Step 1, cut the pipe square.

Use the pipe cutter for a clean, square cut. Deburr the inside of the cut end with the built-in reamer or a small file. The pipe end must be round, undamaged, and fully deburred; a burr inside the pipe prevents the olive seating correctly.
Step 2, slide on the nut and olive.

Slide the nut onto the pipe (threaded end facing toward the fitting), then slide the olive onto the pipe (tapered end toward the fitting for tapered olives). Do not use PTFE tape, jointing compound, or flux on a compression fitting; these products can prevent the olive seating and cause leaks.
Assembly
Step 3, insert the pipe into the fitting.

Push the pipe end fully into the fitting body until it seats. Slide the olive down to meet the fitting body and thread the nut onto the fitting body by hand. Tighten the nut to hand-tight so the olive is seated but not yet compressed.
Step 4, tighten the nut.

Hold the fitting body firmly with water-pump pliers or a second spanner so it does not rotate. Tighten the nut with the adjustable spanner by one and a quarter turns from hand-tight. The precise amount is critical: on a 15 mm fitting, one and a quarter turns is correct; on 22 mm, one and a half turns. Do not guess; count the turns.
Testing
Step 5, restore supply and check.

Restore the water supply slowly. Check the nut joint for any drips. A tiny weep at the nut: tighten a further eighth of a turn. A steady drip: the olive may be damaged or misaligned. Turn off the supply, undo the nut, inspect the olive, and reassemble with a new olive if necessary.
Common mistakes
Nut was tightened too hard and still leaks.

Over-tightening deforms the olive so it no longer seals evenly. The olive cannot be re-used. Turn off the supply, cut the pipe back past the old olive, fit a new olive and nut from a fresh fitting, and reassemble at the correct torque.
Fitting leaks where the pipe enters the fitting body, not at the nut.

The olive is the wrong size for the fitting, or it was not positioned correctly before tightening. Compression fittings are nominally sized to pipe OD; a 15 mm fitting takes 15 mm OD copper or plastic pipe.
